inner-join.label: INNER JOIN
inner-join.summary: Retrieve matching rows from two tables.

sk1.title: What is an INNER JOIN?
sk1.bodyMarkdown: An **INNER JOIN** in SQL combines rows from two tables based on a related column between them. Only rows with matching values in both tables are included in the result. For example, if you want to see which students are enrolled in which courses, you can join the `students` and `enrollments` tables using the student ID.

sk2.title: INNER JOIN Example
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Suppose you want to list all students and the courses they are enrolled in. You can use an INNER JOIN between `students` and `enrollments`:

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.term
FROM students
INNER JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```

This query returns only students who have at least one enrollment.

sk3.title: How INNER JOIN Works
sk3.bodyMarkdown: The INNER JOIN clause matches rows from the first table with rows from the second table where the join condition is true. If a row in either table does not have a match, it will not appear in the result. This is useful for finding related data across tables, such as which courses belong to which departments, or which students are enrolled in which courses.

q1.title: INNER JOIN Basics
q1.prompt: What does an INNER JOIN return?
q1.hint: Think about what happens if there is no matching value in the other table.

help.concept: INNER JOIN returns only rows where the join condition matches in both tables.
help.hint_1: Rows must have matching values in both tables to appear in the result.
help.hint_2: If a student is not enrolled in any course, they won't appear in a join between students and enrollments.

options.a: All rows from the first table, regardless of matches in the second table.
options.b: All rows from both tables, including unmatched rows.
options.c: Only rows where there is a match in both tables.

q2.title: Choosing Join Columns
q2.prompt: Which pairs of columns would you use to join these tables with an INNER JOIN? (Select all that apply.)
q2.hint: Look for columns that represent the same kind of data in both tables.

help.concept: To join tables, use columns that represent the same entity, such as IDs and foreign keys.
help.hint_1: Foreign keys in one table usually reference primary keys in another.
help.hint_2: Names and titles are not reliable join columns; IDs are better.

q3.title: Order the INNER JOIN Query
q3.prompt: Arrange the SQL keywords to form a correct INNER JOIN statement to get student names and course titles for all enrollments.
q3.hint: Start with SELECT, then FROM, then JOINs.

help.concept: SQL queries typically start with SELECT, followed by FROM, then JOIN clauses.
help.hint_1: The FROM clause comes right after SELECT.
help.hint_2: JOIN clauses come after FROM and use ON to specify the join condition.

tokens.t3: INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id
tokens.t4: INNER JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id

q4.title: Complete the INNER JOIN
q4.prompt:
Fill in the blank to join `students` and `enrollments` on the student ID:

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.term
FROM students
_____ enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```
q4.hint: Which JOIN type only returns matching rows from both tables?

help.concept: the missing term is used to return only rows with matches in both tables.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN would include unmatched rows from the left table.
help.hint_2: CROSS JOIN creates all possible combinations, not just matches.

q5.title: Write an INNER JOIN Query
q5.prompt: Write a SQL query to list all course titles and their department names.
q5.hint: Join the courses table with departments using the department_id.

help.concept: To get related data from two tables, use INNER JOIN with the correct ON condition.
help.hint_1: Use courses.department_id and departments.id in your ON clause.
help.hint_2: Select the course title and department name columns.

q5.starterCode:
-- List course titles and their department names
SELECT
